verses.text: Joseph falls on his father's face, weeps, and kisses him. He commands the physicians of his household to embalm Israel — the only Hebrew patriarch embalmed Egyptian-style. Forty days for embalming, then seventy days of mourning. Egypt grieves with him.

Joseph falls on his father's face, weeps, and kisses him. He commands the physicians of his household to embalm Israel — the only Hebrew patriarch embalmed Egyptian-style. Forty days for embalming, then seventy days of mourning. Egypt grieves with him.

Joseph falls on his father's face, weeps, and kisses him. He commands the physicians of his household to embalm Israel — the only Hebrew patriarch embalmed Egyptian-style. Forty days for embalming, then seventy days of mourning. Egypt grieves with him.

After the mourning, Joseph asks Pharaoh's household: speak in his ears for me. My father made me swear to bury him in his own grave in Canaan. Let me go up, bury him, and return. Pharaoh: go up, and bury thy father, as he made thee swear. Even a king honors a dying father's oath.

After the mourning, Joseph asks Pharaoh's household: speak in his ears for me. My father made me swear to bury him in his own grave in Canaan. Let me go up, bury him, and return. Pharaoh: go up, and bury thy father, as he made thee swear. Even a king honors a dying father's oath.

After the mourning, Joseph asks Pharaoh's household: speak in his ears for me. My father made me swear to bury him in his own grave in Canaan. Let me go up, bury him, and return. Pharaoh: go up, and bury thy father, as he made thee swear. Even a king honors a dying father's oath.

Joseph goes up to bury his father, and with him all of Pharaoh's servants, the elders of his house, the elders of Egypt, all of Joseph's house, his brothers, and his father's house — only the little ones, flocks, and herds stay in Goshen. Chariots and horsemen go with them; it is a very great company. The patriarch is buried with the dignity of an emperor.

Joseph goes up to bury his father, and with him all of Pharaoh's servants, the elders of his house, the elders of Egypt, all of Joseph's house, his brothers, and his father's house — only the little ones, flocks, and herds stay in Goshen. Chariots and horsemen go with them; it is a very great company. The patriarch is buried with the dignity of an emperor.

Joseph goes up to bury his father, and with him all of Pharaoh's servants, the elders of his house, the elders of Egypt, all of Joseph's house, his brothers, and his father's house — only the little ones, flocks, and herds stay in Goshen. Chariots and horsemen go with them; it is a very great company. The patriarch is buried with the dignity of an emperor.

They reach the threshing floor of Atad beyond the Jordan and mourn with a great and grievous lamentation; Joseph keeps seven days of mourning for his father. The Canaanites see and say: this is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians. They name the place Abel-mizraim — meadow of Egypt.

They reach the threshing floor of Atad beyond the Jordan and mourn with a great and grievous lamentation; Joseph keeps seven days of mourning for his father. The Canaanites see and say: this is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians. They name the place Abel-mizraim — meadow of Egypt.

His sons do as he commanded: they carry him into Canaan and bury him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field Abraham bought for a possession of a buryingplace. The patriarch is laid with Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah, Leah — the family completed in death.

His sons do as he commanded: they carry him into Canaan and bury him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field Abraham bought for a possession of a buryingplace. The patriarch is laid with Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah, Leah — the family completed in death.
Joseph returns to Egypt — he, his brothers, and all who had gone up with him to bury his father. The funeral done, ordinary life resumes. Even mourning has its hour and its end.

When the brothers see their father is dead, fear returns: Joseph will hate us; he will repay us for all the evil we did to him. They send a message: your father commanded before he died — forgive the trespass of your brothers, their sin, for they did you evil. Forgive the servants of the God of your father. Joseph weeps when they speak. The brothers cannot yet believe his earlier forgiveness was real.

When the brothers see their father is dead, fear returns: Joseph will hate us; he will repay us for all the evil we did to him. They send a message: your father commanded before he died — forgive the trespass of your brothers, their sin, for they did you evil. Forgive the servants of the God of your father. Joseph weeps when they speak. The brothers cannot yet believe his earlier forgiveness was real.

When the brothers see their father is dead, fear returns: Joseph will hate us; he will repay us for all the evil we did to him. They send a message: your father commanded before he died — forgive the trespass of your brothers, their sin, for they did you evil. Forgive the servants of the God of your father. Joseph weeps when they speak. The brothers cannot yet believe his earlier forgiveness was real.
Then the brothers themselves come and fall down before his face: behold, we are your servants. The dream of the sheaves is fulfilled the final time — but now in repentance, not rivalry.

Joseph speaks softly: fear not. Am I in the place of God? You thought evil against me, but God meant it for good — to bring it to pass as it is this day, to save many people alive. Fear not — I will nourish you and your little ones. He comforted them, and spoke kindly to their hearts. The grace of Genesis lands its final line on the brothers' bowed shoulders.

Joseph speaks softly: fear not. Am I in the place of God? You thought evil against me, but God meant it for good — to bring it to pass as it is this day, to save many people alive. Fear not — I will nourish you and your little ones. He comforted them, and spoke kindly to their hearts. The grace of Genesis lands its final line on the brothers' bowed shoulders.

Joseph speaks softly: fear not. Am I in the place of God? You thought evil against me, but God meant it for good — to bring it to pass as it is this day, to save many people alive. Fear not — I will nourish you and your little ones. He comforted them, and spoke kindly to their hearts. The grace of Genesis lands its final line on the brothers' bowed shoulders.

Joseph dwells in Egypt — he and his father's house. He lives one hundred and ten years. He sees Ephraim's children to the third generation; the children of Machir, Manasseh's son, are placed on Joseph's knees. The favored son becomes a great-grandfather.

Joseph dwells in Egypt — he and his father's house. He lives one hundred and ten years. He sees Ephraim's children to the third generation; the children of Machir, Manasseh's son, are placed on Joseph's knees. The favored son becomes a great-grandfather.

Joseph tells his brothers: I die. But God will surely visit you and bring you out of this land into the land He swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob. The promise outlives every patriarch who carried it.

Joseph takes an oath from the children of Israel: God will surely visit you — you shall carry my bones up from here. The Hebrews will keep this oath for four hundred years. Faith reaches across centuries.

So Joseph died, one hundred and ten years old. They embalmed him and put him in a coffin in Egypt. Genesis ends in a box, in a foreign country — but with a promise that the box will one day go home. Exodus has already begun.
